title: First Explicit Reciprocity Law for Unitary Friedberg—Jacquet Periods
speaker: Murilo Zanarella
abstract:
In the early 2000's, Bertolini and Darmon introduced a new technique to bound Selmer groups of elliptic curves via level raising congruences. This was the first example of what is now termed a "bipartite Euler system", and over the last decade we have seen many breakthroughs on constructing such systems for other Galois representations, including settings such as twisted and cubic triple product, symmetric cube, and Rankin—Selberg, with applications to the Bloch—Kato conjecture and to Iwasawa theory.
For this talk, I'll consider Galois representations attached to automorphic forms on a totally definite unitary group U(2r) over a CM field which are distinguished by the subgroup U(r) x U(r). I'll discuss a new "first explicit reciprocity law" in this setting and its application to the corresponding Bloch—Kato conjecture, focusing on new obstacles which arise from the lack of local multiplicity one.

proof_stages.label: Unitary Friedberg-Jacquet periods and the relative-Langlands criterion
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a CM extension, Hermitian spaces, the spherical pair H inside G, and recalls the conjectural criterion that H-period nonvanishing forces the central standard L-value of the base change to be nonzero and the base change to be symplectic.

proof_stages.label: Bloch-Kato rank-zero target and admissible hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: For cohomological automorphic representations with associated Galois representations, the main theorem is framed as evidence for the rank-zero Bloch-Kato implication from the period nonvanishing, under positivity, relevance, big-image, homological genericity, and related admissibility assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Bipartite Euler-system reduction to auxiliary local classes
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to constructing cohomology classes satisfying the Bloch-Kato local conditions away from an auxiliary prime but having controlled nonzero image in the local quotient at that prime, after which global duality yields Selmer bounds.

proof_stages.label: Nearby Shimura varieties and Abel-Jacobi classes
proof_stages.summary: The desired Euler-system classes are proposed as Abel-Jacobi images of special cycles on a nearby unitary Shimura variety obtained by changing the Hermitian space at an auxiliary prime so that ramification at that prime can occur.

proof_stages.label: Level-raising comparison with functions on the original Shimura set
proof_stages.summary: An admissible level-raising prime gives an isomorphism between localized middle cohomology of the nearby Shimura variety and functions on the original definite Shimura set, where the H-period appears as a distinguished distribution.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Abel-Jacobi map as special-fiber intersection numbers
proof_stages.summary: Using integral models and the geometry of the basic locus, the relevant Abel-Jacobi or residue map is computed by intersection numbers with special-fiber components indexed by Shimura sets, giving the explicit reciprocity map to be matched with the period.

proof_stages.label: Failure of the naive special cycle from local multiplicity
proof_stages.summary: The natural naive cycle has the correct away-from-p behavior but, for r>1, its local p-factor lies in a higher-rank spherical Hecke module and spans a different line from the period, so the naive construction never proves the desired reciprocity law in higher rank.

proof_stages.label: Perturbed derived cycles and the completed explicit reciprocity law
proof_stages.summary: By applying suitable Hecke translates and replacing integral models with derived integral models, the speaker obtains computable derived intersection numbers whose Hecke span contains enough spherical functions to realize the period, completing the explicit reciprocity law needed for the Euler-system argument.

examples.name: Odd symmetric power of a non-CM modular form
examples.note: The speaker gives symmetric powers of the Galois representation attached to a non-CM modular form as a concrete family where the admissibility hypotheses can be checked, illustrating rather than motivating the technical conditions.
examples.engagement_reason: cited as a concrete admissibility example—the odd symmetric power of a non-CM modular form—but no verification or computation is carried out on it.

examples.name: Transverse intersection model for derived cycles
examples.note: A hypothetical transverse intersection of subvarieties is used to make concrete how replacing integral models by derived integral models turns ordinary intersections into derived intersections.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explains how, in the transverse-intersection model, one forms derived integral models by taking integral models of the pieces and derived intersections, then uses these to compute intersection numbers.
